title: Price Direction of Options

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: How do European and American option prices change when each input variable changes (with all others held fixed)?
rules: Call payoff: max(S - K, 0). Put payoff: max(K - S, 0).
European: only exercisable at T. American: exercisable any time before T.
Inputs: S (stock), K (strike), tau = T - t (time to maturity), sigma (volatility), r (rate), D (dividends).
setup.question: Match each input variable's increase to the direction of change in each option price.

learningObjectives: Reason about call vs put price direction as each input moves.
Recognize that volatility ALWAYS raises both call and put prices (more dispersion = more upside for limited-downside payoffs).
See that time-to-maturity has an AMBIGUOUS effect on European calls in the presence of dividends.

stages.label: Stock price S goes UP
stages.prompt: Which option types' prices INCREASE when the stock price S rises?
stages.explanation: Calls profit when S rises (intrinsic value max(S - K, 0) increases). Puts profit when S falls (max(K - S, 0) increases when S decreases).

stages.label: Volatility sigma goes UP
stages.prompt: Which option types INCREASE when volatility rises?
stages.explanation: ALL options rise with volatility. Both calls and puts have unlimited upside but limited downside (option premium can't go below 0). More dispersion in S_T -> more chance of large payoffs.

stages.label: Time to maturity for European calls
stages.prompt: When tau (time to maturity) INCREASES, what happens to a European CALL price (with possible dividends D)?

options.text: AMBIGUOUS. Longer tau usually raises the price (more time for upside). But if a large dividend is paid in between, the longer-dated call might be cheaper than the shorter-dated one.

stages.explanation: European calls with dividends can be NON-MONOTONIC in tau. American options are monotone-increasing (you can always replicate the shorter contract by exercising early).

stages.label: Commit the table
stages.prompt: Fill in: when each variable INCREASES, EUROPEAN CALL goes ___
stages.template: Stock price S goes up: __[a]__ (up/down).  Strike K goes up: __[b]__ (up/down).  Volatility sigma goes up: __[c]__ (up/down).  Risk-free rate r goes up: __[d]__ (up/down).  Dividends D go up: __[e]__ (up/down).
stages.explanation: European call: S up -> up; K up -> down; sigma up -> up; r up -> up (PV of K shrinks); D up -> down (effective stock falls). For European put, all these reverse EXCEPT volatility (still up).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why does r UP increase the call price? (PV of K shrinks; equivalent to discounting strike less.)
Why does the time-to-maturity effect on European calls become MONOTONIC if there are no dividends?
Compute: a 1-month call worth $5 vs. a 2-month call worth $4 -- can this ever be arbitrage-free? (Yes, if a large dividend is between the two expiries.)
